general.title: Be gentle with yourself, {{name}} 💙

1.message: Don't force it today. A gentle practice to wake up the body calmly.

general.title: You're on fire, {{name}}! 🔥
general.message: {{streak}} consecutive days out of {{target}}! Energy is stable at {{energyAvg}}/5. Let's keep this rhythm.

general.message: Top indicators (Energy: {{energyAvg}}/5). Use this moment for Deep Work sessions.

general.title: Slow down, {{name}} 🌙
general.message: Your sleep marks {{sleepAvg}}/5. This is the ideal time for a deep relaxation session.

general.title: Your moment, {{name}} 🧘
general.message: Optimal balance. Your energy is stable. Hold the course in this phase.

checkin.title: How are you feeling today?

checkin.prompt_energy_streak: You had great energy yesterday! Kept it up?

onboarding.subtitle_1: Your personal path to rewire mind and body.
onboarding.title: Welcome to FlowGym
onboarding.feature_privacy_desc: All your data stays on device.

skill.notification_body: You postponed the activity. Time to finish it!

notifications.morning_body: Take 5 minutes for yourself. Recalibrate, recharge and get back to your rhythm. Do your daily check-in!
notifications.lunch_body: Take your eyes off the screen, eat calmly and breathe. The afternoon awaits you.
notifications.backup_body: Don't break your streak. Dedicate 5 minutes to yourself before evening.
notifications.evening_body: Disconnect from socials, breathe and prepare for deep sleep. Your tomorrow starts now.
notifications.missing_checkin_body: Don't break your streak. Take 5 minutes for yourself before the evening.

mind_box_breathing.title: Box Breathing
mind_box_breathing.description: Navy SEALs protocol to activate the parasympathetic system. Reduces acute stress and lowers heart rate.

steps.0: Sit in a comfortable position, with your back straight.
steps.1: Inhale slowly through your nose counting to 4.
steps.2: Hold your breath with full lungs counting to 4.
steps.3: Exhale slowly through your mouth counting to 4.
steps.4: Hold your breath with empty lungs counting to 4.
steps.5: Repeat this cycle continuously and smoothly.

mind_physiological_sigh.title: Physiological Sigh (Huberman Lab)
mind_physiological_sigh.description: Strong clinical evidence in immediately shifting the autonomic nervous system from sympathetic to parasympathetic. Clears excess carbon dioxide.

steps.0: Take a quick, deep inhale through the nose until your lungs are almost totally full.
steps.1: When almost at the limit, take a second brief 'sniff' of air through the nose to maximize alveolar volume.
steps.2: Exhale very slowly and deeply through the mouth until completely empty.
steps.3: Focus on the long outward breath: this is what lowers heart rate.
steps.4: Repeat for 3-5 cycles. This quick hack instantly modulates arousal levels.

body_desk_opening.title: Thoracic Opening for Desk Workers
body_desk_opening.description: Counteracts the 'computer posture' by mechanically inhibiting the tension of the pecs and activating the rhomboids, promoting greater brain oxygenation.

steps.0: Stand up, feet shoulder-width apart.
steps.1: Interlace your fingers behind your back, near your glutes.
steps.2: Straighten your arms and gently push your hands down and away from your body.
steps.3: Open your chest upwards and take 5 deep breaths.
steps.4: Release and rotate your shoulders backward 5 times.

focus_nsdr.title: NSDR (Non-Sleep Deep Rest)
focus_nsdr.description: Deep relaxation protocol without sleep. Increases striatal dopamine by 65% helping cognitive recovery without sleeping.

steps.0: Lie down on a comfortable surface with your arms by your sides.
steps.1: Close your eyes and bring attention to your breath, without forcing it.
steps.2: Perform a mental scan: relax starting from your feet, up to your calves, thighs, pelvis, abdomen.
steps.3: Relax your shoulders, arms, neck, jaw, and the muscles around your eyes.
steps.4: Stay focused on the feeling of weight of your body for the entire duration.
steps.5: When finished, slowly move your fingers and toes and open your eyes.

prod_pomodoro_sprint.title: Micro Focus Sprint (Pomodoro 10m)
prod_pomodoro_sprint.description: Reduces the initial friction (cognitive limit of starting a task). After 5 minutes, the amygdala reduces the psychological resistance to work.

steps.0: Physically close every app/tab not necessary for the single micro-task.
steps.1: Move your phone out of sight (off the desk).
steps.2: For 10 minutes, work only on the single identified task.
steps.3: Don't worry about finishing the task, just worry about not interrupting the action for these 10 minutes.

mood_gratitude_journaling.title: Specific Gratitude (CBT)
mood_gratitude_journaling.description: Positive Psychology Protocol (Martin Seligman). Proven exercise to increase serotonin and reduce baseline cortisol levels over time.

steps.0: Find pen and paper.
steps.1: Don't write generic things ('my family'). Force yourself to find 3 SPECIFIC moments from yesterday or today.
steps.2: Ex: 'The taste of coffee this morning while looking outside' or 'The compliment from [Name]'.
steps.3: Write WHY you are grateful for each of those specific things.
steps.4: Re-read the 3 sentences before finishing.

body_neck_mobility.title: Cervical Mobility Reset
body_neck_mobility.description: Releases the tension of the suboccipital muscles related to visual stress from screens (which cause tension-type headaches).

steps.0: Sitting or standing, slowly lower your chin to your chest (hold for 15 sec).
steps.1: Create slow half-moons: bring your right ear to your right shoulder.
steps.2: Rotate slowly down to the center and bring your left ear to your left shoulder.
steps.3: Do 5 fluid rotations.
steps.4: Look at a point as far away as possible from the window (to relax visual accommodation).

focus_cognitive_dump.title: Cognitive Emptying (Braindump)
focus_cognitive_dump.description: Zeigarnik Effect: the brain obsessively remembers unfinished tasks. Writing them transfers the cognitive load from working memory to paper.

steps.0: Take a blank sheet of paper or open a text file.
steps.1: Quickly write down EVERYTHING that crosses your mind: tasks, worries, ideas, calls to make.
steps.2: Do not filter, do not prioritize, just write a bulleted list for 3 uninterrupted minutes.
steps.3: In the end, put an asterisk only next to the 2 vital things for today. Forget the rest for now.

mood_cognitive_reframing.title: Cognitive Reframing (CBT)
mood_cognitive_reframing.description: Essential cognitive-behavioral technique. Interrupts the cycle of rumination by analyzing the thought as a hypothesis and not as a fact.

steps.0: What is the negative or intrusive thought you are having now? Write it down in one line.
steps.1: Examine the evidence for: why do you think it's irrefutably true?
steps.2: Examine the evidence against: what objective facts show that it might NOT be 100% true?
steps.3: Create a 'Balanced Alternative Thought': rewrite the situation in the most objective and neutral way possible (no catastrophizing).

body_20_20_20.title: 20-20-20 Visual Rule
body_20_20_20.description: Preventive ophthalmological protocol. Reduces ciliary stress (eye muscle spasms) responsible for computer fatigue.

steps.0: Look away from the screen.
steps.1: Stare at an object located at least 6 meters (20 feet) away.
steps.2: Keep your gaze relaxed on that object for 20 seconds.
steps.3: Close your eyes tightly for 2 seconds, and then blink quickly 5 times to rehydrate the cornea.
steps.4: Now do some neck stretching before returning to work.

mind_4_7_8.title: 4-7-8 Breathing (Dr. Weil)
mind_4_7_8.description: Based on ancient Indian pranayama. It has been clinically proven to slow heart rate, reduce performance anxiety, and promote sleep.

steps.0: Place the tip of your tongue behind your upper front teeth and keep it there.
steps.1: Exhale completely through your mouth making a whoosh sound.
steps.2: Close your mouth and inhale quietly through your nose for 4 seconds.
steps.3: Hold your breath (without tightening your throat) for 7 seconds.
steps.4: Exhale with a whoosh from your mouth measuring a full 8 seconds.
steps.5: Repeat for a total of 4 full cycles.

prod_implementation_intentions.title: Implementation Intention (If-Then)
prod_implementation_intentions.description: Based on Peter Gollwitzer's studies (1999). By formulating 'if... then...' plans, the probability of procrastination is reduced by linking the task to an environmental trigger.

steps.0: Think of a task you keep procrastinating today.
steps.1: Define the exact action to be completed in an ultra-specific way.
steps.2: Now build a physical if-then limit.
steps.3: Ex: 'IF I finish having lunch (trigger) / IF it's 3:00 PM, THEN I will sit down, open email X and spend 5 minutes on it'.
steps.4: Write your rule and set it as a visual reminder or alarm on your phone.

body_wall_sits.title: Isometric Wall Squat (Wall Sit)
body_wall_sits.description: Isometric exercise stimulates pain receptors lowering post-exertion blood pressure, improving blood flow to the brain and 'resetting' chemical focus.

steps.0: Find a clear wall.
steps.1: Lean against the wall sliding down as if sitting on an invisible chair.
steps.2: Your knees should form a 90-degree angle. Plant your heels firmly on the ground.
steps.3: Contract your abs. Try to hold the position for 45 - 60 seconds.
steps.4: Rest for 30 seconds and repeat 2 more times. Focus on the burn and your breath.

mind_five_senses.title: Grounding 5-4-3-2-1
mind_five_senses.description: Clinical technique to anchor oneself to the present (Grounding). Blocks latent panic attacks by shifting neurological processing from the prefrontal cortex to sensory centers.

steps.0: Look around and name out loud 5 physical things you CAN SEE.
steps.1: Touch and acknowledge 4 things you CAN FEEL physically (the texture of the desk, your clothes).
steps.2: Listen and accurately identify 3 distinct sounds you CAN HEAR.
steps.3: Make an effort to find 2 smells you CAN SMELL (if there are none nearby, smell a book, your wrist, etc).
steps.4: Identify 1 thing you CAN TASTE (Drink a sip of water or notice the current taste in your mouth).

focus_progressive_muscle.title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation (Jacobson)
focus_progressive_muscle.description: Developed by Dr. Edmund Jacobson in the 1920s. Helps physically recognize the somatic difference between tension and relaxation.

steps.0: Sit comfortably with your eyes closed.
steps.1: Starting from your FEET: contract your toes and calves as hard as possible for 5 seconds.
steps.2: Release the tension suddenly and notice the feeling of warmth (10 sec pause).
steps.3: Move to THIGHS AND GLUTES: contract for 5 sec, then release.
steps.4: Move to ABDOMEN AND CHEST: hold and contract for 5 sec, then exhale and release.
steps.5: Move first to ARMS/HANDS (clench fists), then to SHOULDERS/NECK, finally to FACIAL MUSCLES.
steps.6: Breathe normally for the last minute remaining passively relaxed.

prod_eat_the_frog.title: Extracting the Toad (Task Chunking)
prod_eat_the_frog.description: Crosses the Eisenhower Matrix with micro-chunking. Isolates the task with the highest anxiety rate to disarm its cognitive paralysis.

steps.0: Identify which is today's task that causes you the most stress and that you would be about to avoid (The Toad).
steps.1: Stop thinking about the whole project. What is the literal VERY FIRST MEASURABLE STEP in seconds to take?
steps.2: Ex: 'I have to do my taxes' -> First step: 'Open the PDF folder'.
steps.3: Write down or declare to yourself ONLY THAT MICRO-STEP.
steps.4: Perform the ridiculous action of that micro-step. Once done, inertia will drag you through the next steps.

body_vagus_nerve_massage.title: Vagus Nerve Stimulation
body_vagus_nerve_massage.description: The vagus nerve connects the gut, heart, and brain. Gentle physical maneuvers can send calming chemical inputs to the brainstem (increased vagal tone).

steps.0: Interlock your hands behind your head, at the base of the skull.
steps.1: Keeping your neck still and your head straight, shift your gaze only with your eyes to the right.
steps.2: Maintain the extreme right gaze until a spontaneous sigh or yawn or swallowing occurs (about 30-60 sec).
steps.3: Bring your eyes back to the center and rest for 5 seconds.
steps.4: Repeat looking extreme to the left until physiological relaxation manifests.

prod_two_minute_rule.title: The 2-Minute Rule
prod_two_minute_rule.description: Borrowed from 'Getting Things Done' (David Allen). Helps instantly clear the debt of unresolved micro-tasks regaining control.

steps.0: Set a total timer of 5 minutes (of which 1 for planning, 4 for execution).
steps.1: Quickly rethink pending things that can literally require less than two minutes. (e.g., answer that quick chat, throw away paper, wash the cup).
steps.2: In the remaining 4 minutes, execute like a machine as many of these micro-tasks as you can without thinking.
steps.3: Stop relentlessly when the timer goes off. The dopamine spike from mini-victories will reset your brain.

extra_meditation.title: Mindfulness Meditation
extra_meditation.description: Practice based on MBSR protocols (Jon Kabat-Zinn). A meta-analysis of 47 studies (JAMA 2014) shows significant reduction of anxiety, depression, and chronic pain.

steps.0: Sit in a comfortable position, back straight, hands on your knees.
steps.1: Close your eyes and bring attention to your natural breath.
steps.2: When your mind wanders (and it will), notice the thought without judgment and bring your attention back to the breath.
steps.3: Do not force: observe every inhalation and exhalation as if it were a wave.
steps.4: If you get distracted 100 times, bring yourself back 100 times — that's the muscle you're training.
steps.5: When finished, slowly open your eyes and notice how you feel.

extra_baduanjin.title: Ba Duan Jin (Eight Brocades)
extra_baduanjin.description: An 800-year-old Chinese Qigong sequence. Clinical studies (Journal of Clinical Medicine, 2020) demonstrate improvements in balance, flexibility, and oxidative stress reduction. Each exercise should be repeated 8 times.

steps.0:
HOLDING THE HEAVENS WITH BOTH HANDS
• Movement: Feet shoulder-width apart. Hands rise in front of the body, palms turn upward. Push toward the sky, stretching the entire body.
• Explanation: Lengthen the spine, open the chest, and breathe deeply. Then relax and bring hands back down.
• Purpose: Regulates breathing, stretches the body and activates internal energy.
• Repeat 8 times.
steps.1:
DRAWING THE BOW AS IF SHOOTING A HAWK
• Movement: Horse stance. One hand simulates the bow, the other pulls the string. Look in the direction of the arrow.
• Explanation: Open the chest, keep the back straight. Alternate right and left sides.
• Purpose: Strengthens arms and chest, improves posture.
• Repeat 8 times (4 per side).
steps.2:
SEPARATING HEAVEN AND EARTH
• Movement: One hand rises above the head, the other pushes downward.
• Explanation: Both hands push in opposite directions. The body remains centered.
• Purpose: Stretches the sides of the body, stimulates stomach and spleen.
• Repeat 8 times (4 per side).
steps.3:
LOOKING BACK TO PREVENT FATIGUE
• Movement: Hands relaxed at the sides. Slowly rotate the head looking behind.
• Explanation: The movement is slow and controlled. The body stays still.
• Purpose: Relaxes the neck, improves energy circulation to the head.
• Repeat 8 times (4 per side).
steps.4:
SWINGING THE HEAD AND TAIL
• Movement: Horse stance. Move the torso from side to side.
• Explanation: The pelvis moves slightly. The head follows the movement.
• Purpose: Relaxes the lumbar spine, reduces tension and stress.
• Repeat 8 times.
steps.5:
TOUCHING THE FEET WITH BOTH HANDS
• Movement: Bend the torso forward. Hands toward the feet.
• Explanation: Don't force it. The movement should be fluid.
• Purpose: Stretches the back, stimulates the kidneys.
• Repeat 8 times.
steps.6:
CLENCHING FISTS WITH FIERCE GAZE
• Movement: Horse stance. Punch forward with energy.
• Explanation: The gaze is intense. The body remains stable.
• Purpose: Strengthens muscles and determination.
• Repeat 8 times (4 per side).
steps.7:
RISING ON TIPTOES AND DROPPING DOWN
• Movement: Rise on the tips of your feet. Then relax and let the heels drop.
• Explanation: Soft movement. Repeat multiple times.
• Purpose: Stimulates circulation, relaxes the entire body.
• Repeat 8 times.

extra_microlearning.title: Microlearning — Growth Pill
extra_microlearning.description: Based on the Spacing Effect (Ebbinghaus) and micro-learning model. Short and frequent sessions consolidate long term memory better than long sessions.

steps.0: Choose ONE specific concept you want to learn today (not an entire topic).
steps.1: Read or watch specific content for a maximum of 3 minutes (a short article, a short video).
steps.2: Close the source and write down 3 key points you have learned from memory.
steps.3: Ask yourself: "How can I apply this in my life today?"
steps.4: Write the answer in one sentence. This closes the feedback loop and fixes the memory.

extra_box_breathing.title: Box Breathing (Navy SEAL Respiration)
extra_box_breathing.description: Biofeedback technique used for autonomic nervous system control. Normalizes heart rate and reduces cortisol levels under acute stress.

steps.0: Exhale completely your lungs.
steps.1: Inhale slowly from your nose counting to 4.
steps.2: Hold your breath with full lungs for 4 seconds.
steps.3: Exhale deeply from your mouth counting to 4.
steps.4: Keep your lungs empty for 4 seconds before the next cycle.
steps.5: Repeat for the entire duration, maintaining the "square" rhythm.

extra_jacobson.title: Jacobson's Relaxation
extra_jacobson.description: Clinically validated method for reducing residual muscle tension. Effective in treating insomnia and somatic anxiety disorders.

steps.0: Lie down or sit in a quiet place without distractions.
steps.1: Clench your fists tightly for 5 seconds, feel the tension, then release suddenly.
steps.2: Contract your biceps by bringing your hands towards your shoulders, hold, then relax.
steps.3: Contract your facial muscles by squeezing your eyes and lips tightly, then release.
steps.4: Push your feet against the floor by tensing your calves, hold and let go.
steps.5: Notice the deep contrast between the state of contraction and relaxation.

extra_gratitude.title: Gratitude Journal
extra_gratitude.description: Core practice of positive psychology (Emmons & McCullough). Correlated with increased subjective well-being and better sleep quality.

steps.0: Take a notebook or use the app's Logbook.
steps.1: Write down 3 specific events from the last 24 hours that you feel grateful for.
steps.2: For each event, write a short sentence on why it happened.
steps.3: Savor the positive feeling associated with these memories for 30 seconds.
steps.4: This practice trains the brain to shift focus from problems to resources.

prod_zeigarnik_loop.title: Zeigarnik Loop Closure
prod_zeigarnik_loop.description: The Zeigarnik effect states that interrupted tasks hold your mental RAM hostage. Externalizing them instantly lowers anxiety.

steps.0: Grab a pen and paper or open a note on your phone. You physically need to write.
steps.1: Quickly list all 'open loops' (unfinished conversations, pending decisions, emails to send). Everything buzzing in your head.
steps.2: Do not organize or filter the list. The goal is translating the burden from working memory (RAM) to an external drive.
steps.3: Breathe. Your brain now knows the information is safe and will stop obsessively bringing it up.
steps.4: Only now, choose one single real priority for the day.

body_bilateral_stimulation.title: Bilateral Stimulation (Butterfly Hug)
body_bilateral_stimulation.description: Technique used in trauma therapy (EMDR) to process strong emotional responses by de-escalating amygdala hyper-activation via hemispheric interaction.

steps.0: Cross your arms over your chest so your right fingers touch your left collarbone/shoulder and vice versa.
steps.1: Close your eyes and begin to tap alternately on your shoulders, rhythmically (like butterfly wings).
steps.2: Do not try to push away frustrating thoughts or emotions of the moment, let them flow.
steps.3: Continue tapping for one or two full minutes.
steps.4: Stop when you feel a clear drop in emotional intensity in your body or when the urgency of the emotion decreases.

mind_self_compassion_break.title: Self-Compassion Break (Kristin Neff)
mind_self_compassion_break.description: Specific protocol tested on athletes to defuse severe self-criticism in moments of failure or inadequacy that paralyze subsequent action.

steps.0: First step - Mindfulness: openly admit the difficulty. Say to yourself: 'This is a really difficult moment' or 'This hurts right now'.
steps.1: Second step - Common humanity: recognize you are not alone. Think: 'Other people feel this too', 'Feeling inadequate sometimes is normal for humans'.
steps.2: Third step - Kindness: put a hand over your heart for the physical warmth of calming tactile contact.
steps.3: Repeat the healing intention: 'May I be understanding toward myself; may I give myself what I need right now'.
steps.4: Self-criticism activates threat (fight-flight), compassion releases oxytocin promoting the primordial sense of care.

extra_yoganidra.title: Yoga Nidra (NSDR)
extra_yoganidra.description: Dynamic sleep protocol (Non-Sleep Deep Rest). Studies show 20 minutes restores dopamine levels equivalent to a light deep sleep cycle.

steps.0: Lie on your back (Savasana) in a quiet place, with arms and legs slightly apart.
steps.1: Close your eyes and bring awareness to your natural breath.
steps.2: Begin a rotation of awareness: bring attention to the right thumb, index, middle, ring, pinky...
steps.3: Mentally shift attention to each body part without moving it, moving from the right side to the left, down to the feet.
steps.4: Focus on the sensation of heaviness, then on lightness (parasympathetic nervous system stimulation).
steps.5: Finish by gradually awakening the body with small movements before opening your eyes.

extra_cognitive_defusion.title: Cognitive Defusion (ACT)
extra_cognitive_defusion.description: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) exercise to defuse reactivity to intrusive thoughts, creating psychological distance.

steps.0: Identify a recurring negative thought or self-criticism (e.g. 'I am a failure').
steps.1: Notice how it makes you feel in your body when you are 'fused' with this thought.
steps.2: Now, slowly say out loud: 'I am having the thought that I am a failure'.
steps.3: Take a further distancing step: 'I notice that I am having the thought that I am a failure'.
steps.4: Imagine your thought written on a leaf floating down a stream, and watch it drift away.
steps.5: The thought is not you; it is just a transient mental event.

extra_metta.title: Metta Meditation (Loving-Kindness)
extra_metta.description: Loving-kindness practice associated with increased vagal tone and decreased arousal associated with chronic stress and anger.

steps.0: Sit comfortably with your eyes closed and take three deep breaths.
steps.1: Bring attention to the center of your chest. Visualize yourself and mentally repeat: 'May I be happy, may I be safe, may I be healthy'.
steps.2: Bring to mind a loved one and repeat: 'May you be happy, may you be safe, may you be healthy'.
steps.3: Now bring to mind a 'neutral' person (e.g. the mail carrier, a cashier) and dedicate the same phrases to them.
steps.4: Finish by extending the thought: 'May all beings be happy, safe, and healthy'.
steps.5: Notice the sensations of openness and warmth in your body before concluding.
